speaker: interviewer
text: Shlomo Berger, Los Angeles, California, USA. Shlomo, I want to ask you a question. You forgot to tell us, what did you do in this country? What did you start out to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When I came in 1950, I had a family to support. And I had a family support, even though I'd never liked the-- the trade that I learned while I was in home, tailoring. But that was the best choice that I had when I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- I went to work in a factory. I worked for five years in a factory, as a machine operator, in a clothing factory as a machine operator. I became so good at it that, after one year, I became the sample maker. And after being five years here, I figured I can do so good for somebody else, might as well do it for myself. And I opened my own factory. I op-- Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1955. I opened my-- in a small way. I was making ladies clothes. I was doing the designing. I was doing the cutting. Machine people were doing the operating. And I was selling to small-- to all department stores in here, Bullock's, Broadway, the smaller department stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's not small. That's big.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and in no matter how successful I would-- I was going to be, I still didn't-- still didn't like what I was doing. I still felt that I can do more than that, to be involved in something I hate. I always liked books. I liked accounting. I probably would have made a good lawyer, but I never-- I never-- I could never do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, after 10 years, I gave up my shop. My brother and I went in, and we opened a market as a liquor store. Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Michael. That's the only brother I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We opened a liquor store that was very, very small. Within five years, we became so successful that we had eight people working for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that liquor store?

speaker: interviewee
text: The liquor store was in South Central Los Angeles, on Hoover Street. And the-- the-- the business was going so well that we were on the way to become rich. Came in 1965, became the riots, The black riots. They destroyed my business. They took everything away from me. They demolished the store, and I became a poor man all over again. And I had to start all over again to start to build my future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not poor. Not poor mentally, just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Physically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Physically-- not physically--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean financially. And I had to start all over again. We opened up another store in another area. And to be working that for another 15 years. Everything was going fine, until I decided-- I was at the age of 58-- I had enough, and I was going to retire. Being that young, I couldn't take retirement. So I went back to school. I went to West LA college. I took a real estate class. I took a business law class. I took two-- two-- two classes of refreshing accounting. But still my love for selling. I was always liked selling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went into work for real estate. I worked for Fred Sands Realtors in Beverly Hills. And I worked there for 13 years. I was very, very successful in my business. During that time, I got involved in community work. That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged to-- since 1965, I belonged to the B'nai B'rith, the Beverly Hills B'nai B'rith, but I wasn't active until the time when I retired from the liquor business and I had more time. Within three or four years being active, I got elected to be the President of the Beverly Hills B'nai B'rith. I was very successful running that-- running that lodge. I was also on the board of the ADL, Anti-defamation League. I was on the board of Jewish National Fund. I was one of the organizers of the Salute of Israel that's being done every year. And lately-- lately, we collected every, at these shows, $120,000 to $150,000. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: You are also a member of the Washington Museum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm a member of the Washington Museum. I'm a member of the-- of the-- of the museum in-- in-- from the Jewish Federation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martyrs Memorial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Martyrs Memorial. I am also a member of the-- of different types of organizations. But lately, three years ago, I decided I'm going to retire. I don't want to work anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm right now 76.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I-- [NON-ENGLISH], that means a living I have. We have a nice home. It's all paid, no mortgage. I visited Israel a few times. First time I was in Israel in 1968. I came in a year after the 1967 war. I enjoyed that parade immensely. That was an independence parade. But at that time, also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I toured. I toured Israel, and we were on a bus. And the bus driver was-- spoke very nice English, the tour guide. But the certain things I did not like, but I heard. In other words, me being under fascist rules, under rules of dictatorships, I realized that, if you want to have a country, you want to have peace, you must be democratic. You must give everybody else equal rights. And I didn't like what he said. When he saw an Arab riding a donkey, he said, here is a donkey on top of a donkey. He said it in Hebrew. A chamor on top of a chamor. I came to a conclusion--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was ignorant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was ignorant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is-- this is-- at that time, in 1968, this is how they felt about it. 1971, my daughter went to the University of Jerusalem for one year. So we went to visit her, again. And I had plans to liquidate everything here and move to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But seeing what they were saying at that time about the [? protection ?] and all that stuff, I consulted my cousins over there. One cousin is a very rich importer, lived there for about 400 years, the family. And another one is a big lawyer on Ben Yehuda street. And I consulted them both.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one, the lawyer on Ben Yehuda Street, was the chairman of the Labor Department in Jerusalem. So I said, [? Gabi, ?] what do you think if I want to move to Israel? So he says to me, well, Shlomo, he says, if you want to get a job here, I can get you a supervising job any time you want to. He says, but you've got to make you own decisions. I asked the other one, the businessman, what do you think? He says, if you want to come to work here, it's not for you. If you want to come in business, he says, you've got to take in a-- an Israeli partner. And before you look around, he is going to have your business, and he's going to have your money, and you're going to go back to America to work. So I made up my mind at that time that I'm going to stay in America. And I'm going to do whatever I can, and I will support Israel with thought and with money, with help, with anything I can. But I'm going to live in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, to discuss it more, another thing that I didn't like, why I didn't want to go to Israel, because I didn't like the way they treated the minorities. In other words, like I said before, living in a dictatorship, I realized that the only people that live in a dictatorship [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] can rule the people. What Germany, or Ru-- communist Russia, or the Pinochet in South America. But if you want to be a dictatorship, you've got to give people equal rights. You cannot treat people that lived there for many years as second class citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you want to be a democracy--

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to be a democracy. If you don't want to be a democracy, don't say that you are a democracy. And I came to a conclusion, in 1988, the same conclusion that the government of Israel came in 1995, and I expressed that in that book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's right. You wrote a book.

speaker: interviewee
text: I expressed it in the book, that my nephew, which is a professor at the University of Wisconsin, teaches Holocaust. In 1988, in his interview with me, I expressed that opinion that is in that book. That I said that all you have to give the Arabs equal rights, or you have to let them go. You cannot tell them-- you cannot keep him under the yoke. And even though the whole-- this is heresy. In Jewish-- in Jewish-- in Jewish think, it's heresy, but this is how I believe. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- excuse me for interrupting, I agree with you. But it's very unusual for a Betarist, a Betar man, to talk this way. And I'm very proud of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well let's say like this. When I was young--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're a human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was young, I was full of fight. I was-- I was trying to go and fight for an independence of Jews. I knew when the Jews will have a country, they will have a passport, they'll have someplace to go, and they'll have somebody to talk for them. If the Jews would have a country, when Hitler came to power, they wouldn't murder 3 million Jews, or 6 million Jews like they did. But while I was younger was different. I am now an established person with a clear mind. And I don't think any more like a Betari.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think like a human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think like a person. And I was, in 1971, in Israel to visit my daughter. I came to the Knesset. And I said, I want to see Menachem Begin. I knew Menachem Begin from 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had met Jabotinsky in 1937, in Krakow, at the convention. And I said, I want to see Menachem Begin. They say, why do you want to see Menachem Begin. I said, because I know him and he knows me. They took my passport. They went into his office. He came out to the cafeteria. He treated us for lunch, my wife, and me, and my daughter Marlene. He invited us to his house in Tel Aviv for an Oneg Shabbat. And I believe that he-- I believe that he was a very, very good man. But still today, I don't agree with the policy of the Likud. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe in democracy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good for you. Now we're going to see some pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this picture describes the front of my house. There's a sign in front of the house which says Jacob Berger, tailor. On that picture, from the bottom line from the left, the first one in the white shirt is my older brother, Joshua. The second one is my niece, Miriam. She lives here in Los Angeles. And right next to her is her mother, Helena, that died in Berlin. The li-- the--the youngest boy in the-- in the cap, this is Dr. Arnold Gabel. He lives here in Long Beach. The next one is my father, in the hat with the beard. Next to my father is my mother-- my mother Rose and my father Jacob. And below, this little girl is my niece, Sonia. And to the right, the last one to the right, is my brother Mike. The second row to the left is my sister, Berta. Above her is her husband, Rafal. The next one in the cap is my brother, Moses. The next one, next to Moses, is me. And above Moses is a cousin of mine. Her name is Mandel. I don't remember her name. And to the right, the last one to the right, is my sister's sister-in-law, and her name is Herson. And above that, looking out from inside, are her parents, Gabel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. When was it taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken approximately in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1936. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is made sometime in 1934, 1933. It's the sports club, the soccer sports club. It's called Gid-- Gideon, or Gideon. The first one to the left is my older brother, Joshua. Next to him is my younger brother, Michael. And to the right, next to the man in the white shirt, is Shlomo. That's me. The rest are all friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken, Shlomo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Krosno, sometime in 1933 or 1934. This is a picture of me and my wife-- my wife, Gertrude. It was taking approximately 22 years ago, at the wedding of my daughter, Marlene, in Los Angeles, California. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken sometimes in the '60s, in Los Angeles, California. This is a picture of my family, when they were still alive. The first one sitting to the left is my sister, Rose. She is still alive. She's presently 89 years old. The next one to her, to the right, is my sister, Eleanor. She passed away about six years ago. The next one is my sister-in-law, Mildred, which is married to my brother Mike. The next one is my sister, Frances, the one that brought us out to America. She's also passed away. And the-- far to the right is my wife, Gertrude. Standing up, is-- to the left, is my brother Mike, my brother-in-law, Jack Weissbluth, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was taken in the '60s, in Los Angeles, in the '60s. I don't rem-- the '60s or the '70s.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this picture is taken at my grandson, Daniel's, bar mitzvah. And it was three years ago. On the bottom line is my grandson, Alex, my son's son. That was Daniel, the bar mitzvah boy, and then there is Jesse, my daughter's son. And then there's my wife, Gertrude, to the right. Standing to the left, the first one to the left, is my son-in-law-- my son-in-law, Lee. Then there is me. My wife's sister, Mina, lives in New York. The one in the hat is my daughter, Marlene. The next one to her, in the white, is my daughter-in-law, Ethel, and my son, Dr. Jack Berger. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my granddaughter, Tara Berger. And this picture was taken in Los Angeles, I think sometimes in 1992 or 1991, something like that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In this book, which was published by the University of Colorado, and it was written by my nephew, Dr. Ronald Berger, which is a professor of sociology at the University of Wisconsin, Whitewater. He teaches a Holo-- Holocaust class. He interviewed my brother, Michael, and me in 1988. He wrote that book. And it's called of by Ronald J. Berger. The book is going to be published, in general, in the next month or two. And the picture in front is my brother Michael, in front of Auschwitz, in-- sometimes, I think, in 1990 or 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for the interview, Mr. Berger.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
